notes: Reconstructed from "ten".
Reconstructed from "sen".
Reconstructed from prefix "Гә-".
Reconstructed from "phain".
Based on Greek ОјОӯПҒОҝПӮ (mГ©ros), which originally means "part". The idea that "some" represents a part of a larger thing seems logical enough to treat this word as a determiner.
Reconstructed from Gnomish "eleg".
Revised from Noldorin "tГ»g". Homonym to "tЕ·g" (thigh).
Word rejected by Tolkien, but no replacement was defined.
Reconstructed from "Mablung"
Reconstructed from "Caradras dilthen"

definition: man, as a husband, partner, spouse

notes: From вҲҡATATA, "two", "the second"
More formal than "bess", associated with "lady" as the wife of a "lord".
More formal than "benn" or "bethron", associated with "lord".
Standard word to describe any tree.

Word used for excurrent trees, that is, a tree with a strong central leader, giving it a tall, straight, relatively narrow form, such as pines, spruces, firs, poplars, and, to a lesser extent, birches. For decurrent trees, use the standard tree word "galadh".
Ancient variant plural of "orn" reinterpreted a collective word.
Probably a loanword from Nandorin

A neologism to describe a stick as a simple piece of wood, inspired by Germanic languages Stock, stok, stokk, Romance languages palus, pau, palo, par, Finnish keppi, Japanese жңЁгҒ®жЈ’, Chinese жңЁжЈҚ. Conservative speakers may prefer to use "golf".
(There are two possible origins of this word, one is given below, and another one on Quettamorphosis.)
Reconstructed from "Fladrif".
Can refer to a single flower or a group of flowers.
Reconstructed from "Lisgardh".
Taken from "Taras-ness". The meaning is unclear, but it's assumed to mean "meadow" due to the possible root вҲҡNES.
Reconstructed from "talraph". The meaning has been theorised by David Salo. GS/281
Strong, grey and silky fibre made from the inner bark of mallorn trees.
Reconstructed from "Fladrif".
Neologism for "meat", based on Old English term "flesh-meat" (12c), in which "meat" referred to food in general. (French had a similar term, "viande", which meant "food".)
Adapted from Noldorin word "aes", which doesn't follow Sindarin phonetic standards. Some people use the Noldorin variant regardless.
Neologism coined based on Neo-Quenya word.
Reconstructed from "gwaelod".
hidden: Roots seem to work with law+tod

notes: This word predates "laud" because the latter was only published in 2024.

english: head of hair, fax, mass of long hair

notes: The singular form is based on a primitive dual inflection, and the plural is based on the ancient plural, so they don't follow standard pluralisation rules.
The singular form is based on a primitive dual inflection, and the plural is based on the ancient plural, so they don't follow standard pluralisation rules.

This word follows the same pattern of "lhaw" (ears) and "sunt" (nostrils), in which a primitive dual is fossilised into a modern Sindarin word. As a pair of eyes is as natural as a pair of ears or nostrils, it makes sense to have a dual plural for "eye".
Based on Gnomish "gogail", and using the same compound elements from Sindarin.
This word has no direct attestations. Paul Strack suggests using it as part of compounds. For "tooth" noun, use "neleg" instead.
Word coined based on вҲҡNYELEK, which is used for a Neo-Quenya word meaning "fingernail".
Neologism based on Quenya "nappa".
From Gnomish "octha". Word by Sami Paldanius from the VinQuettaParma Wiki.
Element in "Mablung"
Archaic word for "hand". Use mГўb instead, unless if using for poetry or compounds.
This word serves as compund element for "forvo" and "harvo", "right" and "left" sides. So it's implicit it can be used as a sense of "side" as well.

elements.gloss: hold, contain, retain, possess, have in hand

notes: Word indicating a hand holding something, either loosely or tightly.
Neologism based on Quenya "cumbo".
Unclear etymology, but probably from ~ "amboss".
Unclear phonetic development, the Noldorin word is preserved.
Unclear phonetic development, Paul Strack suggested an ancestral word "khikh".

definition: think (in an active and analytical way); reflect
notes: Neologism based on reverse-engineered Quenya "sana-".

definition: ponder, think (in a reflective and contemplative way)
conceive, imagine or create something new with the mind
prick with a sharp point, stab

notes: More conservative users prefer "loda-", meaning "to float". I disagree because "to swim" is one of the 100 words in the Swadesh list and should have its own meaning. Inspired by Latin "nЕҚ" and Ancient Greek "ОҪО¬Пү", which mean "to swim". The best metaphor I can think of is "to move in water" or "to water-move".

definition: sit, to adopt a sitting position
notes: The term in Eldamo "hadh-" doesn't seem to have a logical phonetic evolution, so this word has been revised.

definition: sit, to bring to or place on a seat; cause to sit down

notes: This verb has been argued to collide with prefix "per-" meaning "half", but since they are  different classes of words, I think it's safe to use. The alternative is a possible "perna-".

Gnomish verb for "to turn", but also has a meaning of "to twist". Since we have another verb for "turn", I think this fits better with the idea of "twist", so that's listed as chief meaning. Whatever the case, it's related to a circular motion.
A bit hypothetical because it requires some assumptions about its root for it to work.
Reconstructed from "genediad".
This word is used by more conservative Sindarin users as a word for "to swim". But I don't think that swimming and floating should be synonyms, so I propose a neologism for "to swim".

This is a Noldorin verb. It's Sindarin equivalent would be "seria-". But because this word is heavily associated with Sirion, I think it's easily recognisable. One could pretend it to be an defective case.
Based on "ethir" meaning "outflow".
The primitive word is hypothetical because Tolkien insisted in a form that wouldn't develop following the phonetic rules.
Reconstructed from "Aeluin", "Blue Lake" and "Aelin-uial", "Meres of Twilight".

This word is highly debated. I wouldn't claim that "lith" cannot mean both "ash" and "sand", as natural languages permit stranger polysemies than that. But I do think there's a reasonable linguistic case that this merger is unusually unsupported cross-linguistically and that distinguishing the two concepts would improve the lexicon with very little cost. I have used вҲҡLIT "grit" + вң¶-mДҒ "instrumental suffix", in analogy to вҲҡSAG "bitter, poison" + -mДҒ = вң¶sagmДҒ "poison".

definition: earth, element, dirt, soil; Earth, the world
notes: In this word, the rule that removes the final "-n" from words did not seem to apply, suggesting that either this word has a different origin like "kemenД“" or it's a back-formation from some inflected form. Prefer to use "amar" to refer to the whole world.

definition: Sky, the heavens, firmament, region of the stars

notes: (The phonetic development here is a guess.)
Neologism based on Quenya cognate.
Word coined from Middle Quenya "kuluina".
The root for this word is outdated on Eldamo, so the PE version of the word should be "malinДҒ" instead of "smalinДҒ". (The result is the same, though.)
This word has a dubious phonetic evolution.
Reconstructed from Quenya "coranar".
Reconstructed from Quenya "lГ¶a".
Word extracted from "Borgil".
Extracted from "Nen Hithoel".
This word is a homonym of "laew" meaning "frequent", but their primitive versions are different.
Extracted from "Torhir Ifant"

definition: bad; mean; low in quality, value, or importance; paltry; poor; inferior

notes: Extracted from "Agarwaen"
This word has a peculiar phonetic development even in Noldorin form, which we retain in Sindarin. Regular development would yield "tair".

definition: near; hither, on this side of (side of the speaker)

notes: Word taken from "Eryn Fuir" meaning "North Wood". The meaning for north was striken through. David Salo suggested that the word was derived from a root meaning "right hand". The evidence is sound, but the phonetic development is murky.

definition: right hand, a position or direction indicated by its location to the side of an object or the observer
left hand, a position or direction indicated by its location to the side of an object or the observer

notes: "n(a)" is an allative preposition, indicating movement towards something. It is regularly elided to "n'" before vowels and it triggers soft mutation. Avoid using "n(a)" for meanings of time, unless it's to indicate the end of a waiting period, as in "na vedui", "at last". This preposition should not be reduced before a "lost g."

"mi" triggers soft mutation when the modified noun is indefinite. When the noun is definite, the article appears as a suffix as "min". In the singular, "min" causes mixed mutation, and in the plural, nasal mutation.

definition: of; with, accompanied/provided by, associated/marked with
notes: "na(n)" is a prepositional genitive, used for partitive relationships. It becomes "nan" before vowels and triggers nasal mutation. It indicates a sense of possession or having a certain feature.
"a" is regularly modified to "ah" before vowels and it triggers sibilant mutation.
Word reconstructed from Quenya "qui". This is basically the only word for "if" in Sindarin, thus very popular.

This is an expression to represent an equivalent of English word "because". It's also written "a chaeth i", because there is a group that believes that the preposition "an" should cause nasal mutation on the following word ("caeth"). I think it should cause mixed mutation, therefore "aГұ gaeth".
Word appearing only in the name "MГ®r n'Ardhon", or "Jewel of the World".
Taken from Quenya "yondo". Eldamo suggests using "ion(n)" instead.
Neologism based on French "bГ©tail", from "bestia" + collective "-ail". Literally "animals".
Based on the same construction in Gnomish.
Expression to describe a hair of the body.
Neologism based on Middle Quenya "tiuko".

Neologism based on the word for "centre". Navel is a very conservative word. The best metaphor I could find is for "centre". Ancient Greek "бҪҖОјПҶОұО»ПҢПӮ" (omphalГіs), Arabic "ШіЩҸШұЩ‘Ш©" (surrah), Japanese "гҒёгҒқ" (heso) and Sanskrit "аӨЁаӨҫаӨӯаӨҝ" (nДҒbhi) all seem to imply "centre". Other sources from PIE, Russian, Chinese, Hebrew, etc, seem to all have a native word for navel. So I've picked the root for "centre" and simply applied the phonetic rules, ending up with "ГӘn".
Word reconstructed from Quenya "matta". Both with a fictitious ancient word "mattДҒ" to explain the phonetic changes.
This word's ancestral version was apparently rejected, leaving the state of this word unknown.
This has connotations of overcooked. For regular "cooked", use "brannen".
Past participle of "brath-". For "overcooked", use "brant".
Negated past participle of "brath-". The negative is inspired by proto-Germanic "hrawaz" and Latin "crЕ«dus", both meaning "uncooked".
Negated past participle of "brath-". The negative is inspired by proto-Germanic "hrawaz" and Latin "crЕ«dus", both meaning "uncooked".
Based on Greek ПҺПҒО№ОјОҝПӮ (Гіrimos) "in its proper season", formed by вҲҡIMI + вҲҡMAG + [вҲҡLAW + -stДҒ], literally "in good season". The season part is based on Neo-Quenya "lГ¶asta".

Based on Ancient Greek бј„ПүПҒОҝПӮ (ГЎЕҚros) "not proper season", formed by вҲҡUMU + вҲҡMAG + [вҲҡLAW + -stДҒ], literally "not good season". The season part is based on Neo-Quenya "lГ¶asta". This is a direct equivalent of its antonym "ivelost", also a neologism.
Neologism based on Quenya "mul-".
In Sindarin, [mm] is shortened, so this verb doesn't match its Noldorin counterpart exactly.
Word reconstructed from Quenya "lanne" of the same meaning.
Using same logic as Neo-Quenya "pГЎsГ«" with same meaning.
This is a homonym of the word "caw" meaning "top".
Word based on Neo-Quenya "lancГ¶a", with the same components: "lann" and "caw".
Literal gloss for a cookhouse.
Neologism based on Chinese еҺЁжҲҝ (chГәfГЎng), meaning "cook room". Combining Ancient Telerin "bratК°" and "stambД“".
Reconstructed from Gnomish "nandor".

definition: to till (land), to cultivate; (orig.) to dwell in (a land)
fence, palisade of spikes or sharp stakes

notes: Based on Proto-Germanic "dД«kД…", Latin "fossatum", Proto-Slavic "rovСҠ", Ancient Greek "П„О¬ПҶПҒОҝПӮ", generally all meaning "dug thing". Inspired by "mbazdДҒ" meaning "baked thing".
Based on Quenya "kuv-".
Based on Neo-Quenya "lap-", of same meaning.
Based on Gnomish "botheb-".

definition: to move, to cause to move
notes: Neologism meaning roughly "to cause to move". It's intransitive, thus suggesting moving oneself.

definition: to move, cause something to move
notes: Neologism attempting to translate Quenya "lev-", based on root вҲҡELE.

definition: to move; to push; cause to go
notes: Neologism based on root вҲҡNID to express inprinting movement onto something.

definition: to be in material possession of; to own; to possess as a characteristic quality or attribute
to take, to lay hold of
to seize, take away by force, grasp

notes: Causes soft mutation in the next word.

Neologism inspired by Gnomish, made of an inverted form of the root вҲҡAS beside". According to Paul, this derivation is rather dubious, but he found no better alternatives. This preposition causes soft mutation before consonants and becomes ellided to "s'" before vowels and articles. E.g. "s'en".
Taken from words such as "Iarwain" and "Narwain" and extrapolated from its Noldorin counterpart.
Based on "sГӯr", but using a different word.
Very experimental neologism based on French "flairer", which comes from the idea of "perceiving scent".
Taken from "lissuin"

definition: to astound, make aghast; to surprise, to shock

notes: Neologism based on it opposite "alvar", but changing the prefixed root вҲҡAL(A) meaning "good" for вҲҡOKO meaning "wicked", "bad".
Neologism based on reverse-engineered Quenya "sanar".

definition: to think, to believe, to be of the opinion, to accept as fact

notes: Based on Noldorin "floss", which means "whispering or rustling sound".

Based on the same root used for the Quenya verb "nurru-". I've added an -ДҒ suffix to discern it from "nor-", meaning "to run". There are still some collisions, but I think it's acceptable and the true meaning can be based on context. This word is unlikely to get wide acceptance, but I don't know a better one.

definition: city, fort or dwelling surrounded by bulwarks, circular earthwall with dike
notes: The sense of "earthwall, bulwark" is considered archaic.
Word extracted from Middle Quenya "sanya" (adj).

Neologism coined based on Hebrew Ч‘ЧҷЧӘ ЧһЧ©ЧӨЧҳ (beit mishpat) "house of judgment" and Japanese иЈҒеҲӨжүҖ (saibansho) "place of judgment". The components are "baudh [mb-]" and "bГўr [mb-]", based on Angmar "Iron Home" and skipping the [au] to [o] rule based on "Bauglir [mb-]".
Word linked to "aeda-" meaning "to revere", "to worship".

definition: word used for any god, and probably the Valar, depending on context
notes: Neologism from Gnomish "ain". The original for "aen" collides with another word, so the extended form with the agental suffix "-or" is used instead.

definition: the god said to have created Arda
notes: Based on the same name from Quenya.

definition: holy place, fane, temple, shrine, sanctuary
notes: This is a homonym of adjective "iaun" meaning "wide".
Based on Japanese дҫӣзү©еҸ° (kumotsudai), "offering stand/platform".
Based on Latin "sacrificium", "make sacred". Made by using вҲҡAYA(N) + вң¶-ta (single product of an action) to yield "made reverent" or "made blessed".

Ant is a remarkably conservative word. Germanic languages seem to inherit the word from PIE meaning "the biter-off". This is the only descriptive word for "ant" that I could find, so I've decided to copy the metaphor by using вҲҡPI "small" and coining a "biter" with вҲҡNAK "bite" and -ro.
